## - UNIFYING LIKELIHOOD FREE INFERENCE WITH - BLACK BOX OPTIMIZATION AND BEYOND

**Dinghuai Zhang**[1] _[,]_[2] **, Jie Fu**[1] _[,]_[2] _[∗]_ **, Yoshua Bengio**[1] _[,]_[2] _[,]_[3] **, Aaron Courville**[1] _[,]_[2] _[,]_[3] 1Mila, 2University of Montreal, 3CIFAR Fellow Montreal, Canada _{_ dinghuai.zhang, fujie _}_ @mila.quebec

## ABSTRACT

Black-box optimization formulations for biological sequence design have drawn recent attention due to their promising potential impact on the pharmaceutical industry. In this work, we propose to unify two seemingly distinct worlds: likelihood-free inference and black-box optimization, under one probabilistic framework. In tandem, we provide a recipe for constructing various sequence design methods based on this framework. We show how previous optimization approaches can be “reinvented” in our framework, and further propose new probabilistic black-box optimization algorithms. Extensive experiments on sequence design application illustrate the benefits of the proposed methodology.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Discovering new drugs to fulfill specific criteria, such as binding affinity towards a given molecular target, is a fundamental problem in chemistry and the pharmaceutical industry (Hughes et al., 2011). In this work, we focus on an important subdomain: _de novo_ biological sequence design. This task is challenging for two reasons: (1) the exploration space for sequences is combinatorially large; and (2) sequence usefulness is evaluated via a complicated process which usually involves time-consuming and expensive wet-lab experiments.

Despite the difficulty of this task, many approaches have been developed over the past few decades thanks to recent advances in biochemistry and machine learning. The Nobel Prize wining paradigm, _directed evolution_ (Chen & Arnold, 1991), which conducts local evolutionary search under human guidance, is one of the popular techniques. Unfortunately, it is limited by its sample inefficiency and reliance on strong prior knowledge, _e.g._ , about where to mutate (Ahn et al., 2020). Furthermore, to compete with other machine learning methods (Gottipati et al., 2020), guided evolution (Yoshikawa et al., 2018; Jensen, 2019; Nigam et al., 2019) heavily relies on human intuition for designing domain-specific evolutionary operators, which may not always apply to tasks at hand.

In this work, we deem sequence design to be a black-box optimization problem, tasked with maximizing an unknown oracle function. We assume that oracle queries are limited due to the constraint on resources, such as the budgets for evaluating queries in a wet-lab. Thus, sample efficiency is crucial. We develop a probabilistic framework by reformulating the aforementioned black-box optimization target as a posterior modeling problem. With this framework, we draw a surprising connection between likelihood-free inference and sequence design, and thus linking two fields which are previously considered as unrelated. The key observation we leverage here for establishing this connection is that both settings share similar elements and targets which will be elaborated in Section 2.2. This connection facilitates our understanding of both fields and provides a recipe for developing sequence design algorithms. Going beyond, we also combine different probabilistic modeling insights and develop three novel _composite_ probabilistic algorithms. We point out that our framework could actually be applied to any black-box optimization settings, but in this work we focus on its application to biological sequence design.
To demonstrate the empirical effectiveness of our methods, we conduct systematical experiments to evaluate their performance on four _in-silico_ sequence design benchmarks. Our proposed meth-

ods achieve at least comparable results to existing baselines, and the proposed composite methods behave consistently better than all other ones across various sequence design tasks.

We summarize our contribution as follows:

- We develop a probabilistic framework that unifies likelihood-free inference and black-box optimization.
- Based on this framework, we provide a recipe for designing algorithms for black-box problems. We apply these ideas to propose a series of composite design algorithms.

- We perform systematical evaluation on a series of black-box sequence design benchmarks, and find that these algorithms achieves consistently comparable or better results compared to previous ones, thus illustrating the benefit of the proposed unified framework.

- 2 A UNIFYING PROBABILISTIC FRAMEWORK

- 2.1 BACKGROUND

**Likelihood-free inference (LFI)** . We use _**θ** ∈_ Θ and **x** _∈X_ to separately denote the parameters and the data generated via the mechanism **x** _∼ p_ ( **x** _|_ _**θ**_ ). In this scenario, LFI refers to a special kind of Bayesian inference setting where the likelihood function is not tractable but sampling (by simulation) from the likelihood is feasible. Consider the objective of modeling the Bayesian posterior when we cannot compute the likelihood _p_ ( **x** _o|_ _**θ**_ ):

where **x** _o_ is the observed data, _p_ ( _**θ**_ ) is the (given) prior over the model parameters _**θ**_ , _p_ ( **x** _|_ _**θ**_ ) is the intractable likelihood function and _p_ ( _**θ** |_ **x** ) is the desired posterior over _**θ**_ . While we do not have access to the exact likelihood, we can still simulate (sample) data **x** from the model simulator: **x** _∼ p_ ( **x** _|_ _**θ**_ ). Instead of trying to obtain a numerical value of the generic posterior _p_ ( _**θ** |_ **x** ) for arbitrary **x** , LFI only tries to obtain an approximation of _p_ ( _**θ** |_ **x** _o_ ) for the given **x** _o_ . During the inference process, we can take advantage of the sampled data: _D_ = _{_ ( _**θ** i,_ **x** _i_ ) _}[n] i_ =1[where] **[x]** _[i][∼][p]_[(] **[x]** _[|]_ _**[θ]**[i]_[)][for][selected] values of _**θ** i_ .
**Biological black-box sequence design** . We consider biological sequence design as a black-box optimization problem:

where _f_ ( _·_ ) is the oracle score function, and we would like to discover values of **m** for which _f_ ( **m** ) is large. In real-world situations, a query of this oracle _f_ could represent a series of wet-lab experiments to measure specific chemical properties or specificity for a given binding site target. In general, these experiments are time- and cost-consuming. As a result, the total number of queries is limited.

In our setting, we use _M_ = _V[L]_ to denote the search space for sequences with fixed length _L_ , where _V_ is the vocabulary for each entry of the sequence: for DNA nucleotides _|V|_ = 4, and for protein amino acids _|V|_ = 20. For variable length setting, we have _M_ = _∪L∈_ [ _L_ min _,L_ max] _V[L]_ , where _L_ min and _L_ max are the minimal and maximal length, respectively.

## 2.2 CONNECTING LFI AND BLACK-BOX OPTIMIZATION

In order to draw a connection to LFI, we require a probabilistic formulation of the black-box sequence design problem. To this end, we relax the goal of searching for a single maximum of the oracle / score function _f_ to a posterior modeling problem, _i.e._ , finding a representative sample of the configurations of **m** sampled with probability related to some target posterior. Think of _C_ is the set of sequences with these desirable configurations, _E_ is a Boolean event about whether a sequence **m** belongs to _C_ , and our goal is to characterize the posterior distribution _p_ ( **m** _|E_ ) from which we obtain the desired sequences. Below, we consider two specific ways of doing this:

Here _s_ could be any fixed value, or a certain quantile of a particular score distribution. In this way, we have _p_ ( _E|_ **m** ) = _p_ ( **m** _∈C|_ **m** ) = 1 _{f_ ( **m** ) _≥ s}_ where 1 _{}_ is the indicator function.
**Example B.** In a softer version of _E_ and _C_ , we can define its conditional probability of being true to follow a Boltzmann distribution:

where _τ_ is a temperature parameter. We introduce the exponential because _f_ ( _·_ ) does not necessarily _·_ take positive values. Any monotone transformation of _f_ ( ) to non-negative reals could be used, so that sequences with larger oracle scores have a greater probability of making _E_ true.

With this posterior objective, our goal now becomes effectively modeling and sampling from the posterior _p_ ( **m** _|E_ ). It is thus natural to resort to the tools of Bayesian inference for this task. It can be observed that both tasks share similar elements and targets. The two settings also share similar limitations on the allowed queries, which are too time-consuming and / or cost-intensive. Notice that in sequence design, the oracle could be either exact or noisy, thus we use the more general _s ∼ f_ ( **m** ) formulation rather than _s_ = _f_ ( **m** ). We will further present several concrete examples as demonstrations of this correspondence in the following section.

Another way to understand this correspondence is to consider the following mapping _T_ :

Here we can see the score value _s_ as a quantitative metric for how close the generated data **x** (given _**θ**_ ) is to the target observed data **x** _o_ . In addition, querying the oracle in the sequence design setting can also be thought of as follows: (1) sample **x** _∼ p_ ( _·|_ _**θ**_ ) and then (2) calculate _s_ = _−∥_ **x** _−_ **x** _o∥_ under some distance _∥·∥_ . In this manner, _T_ could conceptually transform any LFI problem into a black-box optimization task. In this work, we only focus on the application of sequence design.

## 3 METHODOLOGY

We provide a recipe for designing new sequence design algorithms based on the correspondence in Section 2.2. The recipe induces different approaches by modeling different probabilistic component of the Bayesian inference problem. We begin with common algorithm restrictions under this setting.

**Common constraint for algorithms** . Due to the restriction of simulation / query in our setting, we constrain our algorithms to act in a sequential / iterative way, gradually achieving the desired posterior round by round. Every algorithm starts with an empty dataset _D_ = ∅ and an initial proposal _p_ 1( _·_ ) = _p_ ( _·_ ), where _p_ ( _·_ ) is the prior given by the task. In the _r_ -th round of this multi-round setting, the algorithm would use the proposal _pr_ ( _·_ ) of this round to sample a batch of data ( _**θ**_ / **m** ) for simulation / query, and augment the current dataset _D_ with the newly obtained batch of data. We use _n_ to denote the batch size for each round’s simulation / query. Afterwards, the algorithm updates

the proposal to _pr_ +1( _·_ ). The outcomes for the two settings we discuss may be slightly different: an algorithm for likelihood-free inference would return the posterior, while a sequence design method would return the dataset of all the sequences it has queried, which hopefully contains desired high scored sequences. On the other hand, a sequence design method could produce as an intermediate result a generative model for sampling queries, which then completely fits with the LFI framework.

## 3.1 BACKWARD MODELING OF THE MECHANISM

Approximate Bayesian Computation (ABC) (Beaumont et al., 2002) is a standard method for tackling LFI problems. In Algorithm 1, we display one of the most popular variants: Sequential Monte Carlo-Approximate Bayesian Computation (SMC-ABC) (Beaumont et al., 2009). In each round, parameters _**θ**_ are sampled from the current proposal distribution _pr_ ( _**θ**_ ) for simulation. A rejection step is then involved to remove the _**θ** i_ whose simulation outcomes **x** _i_ cannot reproduce the observed data **x** _o_ with sufficient accuracy. The remaining accepted _{_ _**θ** i}i_ are adopted to update the next round’s proposal _pr_ +1( _·_ ) towards the target posterior, _i.e._ , by refitting _qφ_ with the modified data. We defer more details of this approach to Section A.1 in Appendix.

It would then be natural to construct an analogical sequence design algorithm using top scored entities _{_ **m** _i}i_ to guide the update of a certain sequence distribution, see Algorithm 2. Interestingly, this is the proposed sequence design algorithm in Gupta & Zou (2019), where the authors name this kind of updating “feedback” because training of the parametric generator _qφ_ ( **m** ) exploits feedback signals from the oracle. In this paper, we follow Brookes & Listgarten (2018) to crystallize _qφ_ ( **m** ) to be a variational autoencoder (Kingma & Welling, 2014), and use the term Feedback-Variational AutoEncoder (FB-VAE) to refer to Algorithm 2. We place Algorithm 1 & 2 side-by-side to highlight their correspondence. We also make the same arrangement for the following Algorithm 3 & 4, Algorithm 5 & 6 and Algorithm 7 & 8.

In comparison with SMC-ABC, the Sequential Neural Posterior (SNP) method (Papamakarios & Murray, 2016; Lueckmann et al., 2017; Greenberg et al., 2019) for likelihood-free inference adopts a more flexible approach, taking the power of conditional neural density estimator ( _e.g._ , Papamakarios et al. (2017)) to model the general posterior _p_ ( _**θ** |_ **x** ), which takes arbitrary _**θ**_ and **x** as two inputs and outputs a distribution. This neural estimator is trained via approximately minimizing the

Kullback–Leibler (KL) divergence between _qφ_ ( _**θ** |_ **x** ) and the true posterior _p_ ( _**θ** |_ **x** ). We defer more training details to Section A.1 in Appendix. Under the connection viewpoint, one similar algorithm for sequence design is the Design by Adaptive Sampling (DbAS) proposed in Brookes & Listgarten (2018) which is characterized in Algorithm 4, fitting _qφ_ ( **m** ) through minimizing the KL divergence with the posterior _p_ ( **m** _|E_ ). Based on the difference in specific implementations, both algorithms have more than one variant, whose details are deferred to Section A.1 in Appendix.

We refer to the above algorithms as “backward modeling” because the trained generative network _qφ_ (going from **x** _/E_ to _**θ** /_ **m** ) is a sort of reverse model of the simulation mechanism (which goes from _**θ** /_ **m** to **x** _/s_ ).

## 3.2 FORWARD MODELING OF THE MECHANISM

Whereas the above methods focus on directly modeling the target posterior with a generative model that learns a “reverse mechanism” of the simulation process, it is also possible to model the “forward mechanism”, which is consistent with the simulation process. Papamakarios et al. (2019) claim that the forward modeling approach may be an easier task than its backward counterpart, as unbiased estimation of the likelihood does not depend on the choice of proposal. Consequently, in contrast to SNP, Papamakarios et al. (2019) chooses to train a neural density estimator to model the conditional likelihood distribution _qφ_ ( **x** _|_ _**θ**_ ) sequentially in each round. ## 3.3 MODELING A PROBABILITY RATIO

In this subsection, we discuss yet another approach, through the estimation of a probability ratio. Gutmann & Hyv¨arinen (2010) proposes noise contrastive estimation as a statistical inference approach. This methodology turns a hard probability modeling problem into binary classification, which is considered easier to learn. In contrast to the aforementioned likelihood-free inference methods which rely on a form of density estimation to perform the task, Sequential Neural Ratio

## 3.4 COMPOSITE PROBABILISTIC METHODS

Building on the above analogies and framework, we move beyond the above algorithms in this subsection. The previous lines of approach – the direct posterior modeling methods in Section 3.1 and the indirect methods in Section 3.2 and 3.3 – both have their own advantages and disadvantages. The former methods may fail to get accurate inference result due to unmatched proposals, while the latter ones would need extra large amount of computation for the MCMC sampling process before obtaining accurate posterior samples, etc. Here we study composite algorithms that combine the aforementioned ingredients through the lens of our proposed unified framework. Our goal is to combine the strengths from both kinds of methods.

We first introduce Iterative Posterior Scoring (IPS) method illustrated in Algorithm 9. IPS also uses a neural network _f_[ˆ] _φ_ ( **m** ) to model the forward mechanism as IS does, and again we use _q_ ˜ here train a second parametrized modelto denote the target posterior _p_ ( **m** _q|Eψ_ ).to modelInstead _q_ ˜ofby minimizing the KL divergence between them.applying computational MCMC steps here, we Notice that the optimization of _qψ_ is restricted within a neural network parameterization family. ## 4 EXPERIMENTS

## 4.1 SETUP

In this section, we systematically evaluate the proposed methods and baselines on four different _in-silico_ biological sequence design benchmarks. In every round, we allow each algorithm to query the black-box oracle for a batch of _n_ sequences **m** _i_ to obtain their true scores _si_ , with _n_ = 100 for all experiments. The total number of rounds differs across different tasks.

Apart from these proposed methods, we consider as baselines a battery of existing methods designed for batched black-box sequence design tasks: (1) Random, a method that randomly select proposal sequences at every round; (2) FB-VAE (Gupta & Zou, 2019) depicted in Section 3.1; (3) Evolution based (Brindle, 1980; Real et al., 2019) sequence design algorithm; and (4) DbAS (Brookes & Listgarten, 2018), described in Section 3.1.

We evaluate these sequence design algorithms by the average score of the top-10 and top-100 sequences in the resulting dataset _D_ at each round. We plot the average score curves with regard to the number of rounds. We also use the area under the curve as a scalar metric for sample efficiency to compare the methods being evaluated. Specifically, since the area depends on the choice of x-axis, we simply cumulate the scores of all rounds to calculate the area. The result could be non-positive, since the score can take negative values.

## 4.2 RESULTS

**Transcription factor binding sites (TfBind)** . Protein sequences that bind with DNA sequences to adjust their activity are called transcription factors. In Barrera et al. (2016), the authors measure the binding properties between a battery of transcription factors and all possible length-8 DNA sequences through biological experiments. Concretely, we choose 15 transcription factors to serve as 15 different tasks. For each transcription factor, the algorithm needs to search for sequences that maximize the corresponding binding activity score. The size of the search space is _|V|[L]_ = 4[8] = 65536. The number of total rounds is fixed to 10. For validation, we follow Angerm¨uller et al. (2020b) and use one task (ZNF200 ~~S~~ 265Y ~~R~~ 1) for hyperparameter selection. Then we test the algorithms’ performance on the other 14 held-out tasks.

This task is similar to the AMP task introduced above, but its ground-truth measurement relies on a regressor. We use a pretrained model taken from Rao et al. More concretely, the regressor is fit on a small neighborhood of parent green fluorescent protein, and is then evaluated on a more distant protein. ## 5 CONCLUSION

We propose a probabilistic framework that unifies likelihood-free inference and black-box optimization for designing biological sequences. This unified perspective enables us to design a variety of novel composite probabilistic sequence design methods combining the best of both worlds. Extensive experiments have demonstrated the benefits of the unified perspective. While the composite probabilistic methods usually outperform other baseline methods in most sequence design tasks we consider in this work, the key contribution of our paper is not just about the superiority of those composite methods, as different specific tasks might prefer different algorithmic configurations due to no free lunch theorem (Wolpert & Macready, 1997). Actually, we would like to attribute the strong performance to the unified probabilistic framework, which enables us to develop a richer algorithm pool, based on which we can design performant algorithms for particular sequence design tasks.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a conceptually novel and ambitious attempt to unify likelihood-free inference (LFI) and black-box optimization under a single probabilistic framework, motivated by applications in biological sequence design. The high-level idea is both intriguing and potentially impactful, as it reframes optimization as posterior modeling, enabling the transfer of tools and insights from Bayesian inference to combinatorial design problems. The writing is generally clear, and the structure follows a logical progression, particularly in Section 2 where the authors carefully lay out the connection between LFI and sequence optimization. However, the paper suffers from several significant issues that undermine its clarity and scientific rigor. First, the core unification argument, while promising, remains underdeveloped in the provided excerpt—specifically, the precise mathematical and conceptual correspondence between the posterior in LFI and the target distribution in optimization is only partially articulated, and the transition from Example A to the general framework feels abrupt and lacks sufficient formal grounding. Second, the paper introduces key concepts (e.g., the probabilistic relaxation of optimization) without adequately justifying why this leads to improved sample efficiency or better exploration compared to existing methods. Third, the experimental evaluation is cited as a strength but is absent in the provided text, limiting confidence in the empirical claims. Additionally, the writing contains minor but recurring grammatical and typographical issues (e.g., “systematical experiments”) that detract from professionalism. Most critically, the paper does not clearly position itself against closely related work in Bayesian optimization, evolutionary strategies, or amortized LFI methods such as neural posterior estimation, raising concerns about novelty and technical differentiation. A more rigorous formal treatment of the proposed equivalence, strengthened theoretical motivation, and clearer comparison to prior work would be essential to support the paper’s high-level claims.

comments.title: Ambiguity in mapping from Boltzmann definition to ratio estimation in IR algorithm
comments.quote: We point out that IR does not have two variants as IS does, as the definition for _E_ in Example B is not usable because of the unknown normalizing constant for probability _p_ ( _E|_ **m** ).
comments.explanation: This claim initially seems contradictory: in Example B, _p_(_E|_**m**) ∝ exp(f(**m**)/τ), which is a Boltzmann-type likelihood. In SNR-style methods like IR, one estimates a density ratio _p_(_m_|_E_) / _p_(_m_), which does not require the normalizing constant of _p_(_E|_**m**)—only the unnormalized likelihood is needed for contrastive learning. Thus, IR should in principle be compatible with Example B by treating high-scoring samples as positives and prior samples as negatives. The authors argue otherwise due to the 'unknown normalizing constant for _p_(_E|_**m_)', but this is a red herring: ratio estimation via classification circumvents normalization entirely. The confusion may arise from conflating the posterior normalization with that of the likelihood. While it's true that _p_(_E|_**m**) must be properly normalized to define _E_ as a probabilistic event, in practice, for classification-based ratio estimation, one only needs relative scores (e.g., exp(f(**m**)/τ)) as a proxy for likelihood. Therefore, the claim that Example B is 'not usable' in IR seems unjustified. The rejection of the second variant may reflect a design choice (e.g., to avoid temperature tuning) rather than a fundamental incompatibility. This is a logical gap: the justification provided is insufficient and contradicts standard practice in likelihood-free inference.

comments.title: Inconsistency in posterior target between text and Algorithm 3 for SNP
comments.explanation: The objective in Algorithm 3 reads: minimize KL(p(θ|x) || q), but this is not standard. In neural posterior estimation (e.g., SNPE or sequential neural posterior), the typical objective is to minimize the KL between the approximate posterior q_φ(θ|x) and the true posterior p(θ|x), which leads to maximum likelihood training on the role-reversed dataset. However, the KL divergence is written here in the reverse direction—KL(p || q)—which is not variationally tractable because p is intractable. This suggests a typo: it should be min_φ KL(q_φ(θ|x) || p(θ|x)), or equivalently, maximizing the log-likelihood E[log q_φ(θ|x)]. The surrounding text says 'minimizing the KL divergence between qφ(θ|x) and the true posterior p(θ|x)', which correctly describes the forward KL. Therefore, the written objective in the algorithm pseudocode contradicts the textual description. This is not an OCR error: 'KL(_p_(**_θ_**_|_**x**)_∥q_)' clearly has _p_ as the first argument. Given that variation inference universally uses KL(q||p), and that optimizing KL(p||q) would require samples from p and is not feasible here, the objective as written is infeasible. The authors likely intended the standard form. This is a genuine formal error in the pseudocode that could mislead implementation.

comments.title: Missing normalization or sampling step in forward modeling (IS and SNL)
comments.quote: The downside of this forward approach is an additional computational Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) step is needed to sample from the _r_ -th round posterior / proposal _pr_ ( _**θ**_ ).
comments.explanation: The text correctly notes that SNL requires MCMC to sample from the posterior due to the lack of an explicit sampler. However, when transitioning to the analog algorithm IS, there is no mention of how _pr_+1(**m**) = ˜_q_(**m**) is sampled from, especially when ˜_q_(**m**) is defined via an unnormalized density (e.g., p(**m**) exp(ˆ_f_φ(**m**)/τ)). The algorithm says 'construct ˜_q_(**m**)' and sets _pr_+1 ← ˜_q_, but offers no detail on how to sample from it in the next round. Unlike SNL, which explicitly uses MCMC, IS appears to assume that sampling from ˜_q_ is straightforward—perhaps via rejection sampling or an implicit generator—but this is not specified. Given that the forward model ˆ_f_φ is a regressor (not a generative model), and ˜_q_ is only defined up to normalization, sampling from it is non-trivial. The omission raises reproducibility concerns: a reader would not know whether to use MCMC, rejection sampling, or another method. While this may be detailed in the appendix (Section A.2), it is underspecified in the main method description. Since sampling is essential to the algorithm's loop, this missing component constitutes a methodological gap.

comments.title: Inconsistent posterior update in Algorithm 4 vs. Algorithm 3
comments.explanation: At first glance, the update in Algorithm 4 appears symmetric to Algorithm 3: both perform density estimation via KL minimization and set the next proposal to the estimated density. However, upon closer inspection, Algorithm 3 updates pr+1(θ) ← qφ(θ|x_o), which conditions on the observed data x_o, while Algorithm 4 sets pr+1(m) ← qφ(m) without conditioning on E. This breaks the correspondence unless qφ(m) is explicitly conditioned on E. The text says that qφ models p(m|E), but since E is not an input to qφ during training or sampling (unlike x in SNP), the model cannot adapt to different events E without retraining. In the LFI setting (Algorithm 3), the neural posterior estimator is trained on (θ_i, x_i) pairs and can evaluate p(θ|x_o) for any x_o; in contrast, Algorithm 4's qφ only sees sequences m_i (and possibly s_i), but not the event E directly. This creates a mismatch: the framework assumes E is fixed during training (e.g., via threshold s in Example A), so the algorithm is not general across different E. While this might be acceptable for fixed tasks, it undermines the claim of a unifying framework unless the conditioning structure is made equivalent. The authors correctly mirror the structure, but the implementation asymmetry weakens the analogy.

comments.title: Ambiguity in probability ratio definition for IR
comments.explanation: The text defines r(m) = p(m|E)/p(m), which by Bayes’ rule should be proportional to p(E|m)/p(E). However, the classifier dφ(m) is trained to distinguish m ∼ p(m|E) from m ∼ p(m), and standard classification theory tells us that the optimal d*(m) = p(D=m|E)/[p(D=m|E)+p(D=m)], assuming equal priors on the two classes. But in IR, D and D′ are not balanced: D contains sequences from the proposal with oracle scores, while D̃ is constructed from sequences satisfying E, and D̃′ is drawn from p(m). The loss in Algorithm 8 does not specify class weights, so the resulting dφ(m) will only recover the density ratio if the training distribution matches the class balance. The text does not clarify whether this balance is enforced. Moreover, in the update pr+1(m) ∝ rφ(m) · p(m), this should yield p(m|E), but only if rφ(m) estimates p(m|E)/p(m) correctly — which depends on the classifier being trained with balanced classes or properly weighted loss. Without such clarification, a reader could misinterpret when the method is valid. Context from SNR helps, but the asymmetry in data construction (D′ is synthetic while D is empirical) is not fully resolved.

comments.title: Claim that backward modeling is superior in Fluo task contradicts LFI literature without explanation
comments.quote: This might signify that for long sequence design tasks, backward modeling is superior to other modeling methods, which is not consistent with LFI (Papamakarios et al., 2019).
comments.explanation: The authors observe that DbAS (a backward modeling method) performs well on the Fluo task and suggest this contradicts the LFI literature (Papamakarios et al., 2019), which claims forward modeling is easier. However, they do not justify why this reversal might occur. While forward modeling (e.g., SNL) avoids dependence on the proposal, backward modeling (e.g., SNP) directly models the posterior, which may be more effective when the posterior is sharp or when the sequence space is extremely high-dimensional (as in Fluo: 20^238). The paper could have discussed potential reasons—e.g., that direct posterior modeling benefits from focusing on high-score regions as the proposal improves, or that the high dimensionality favors amortized posterior estimation. As it stands, the claim is presented as an observation without mechanistic justification, making it feel like a post-hoc rationalization. Given that this is a key insight in the conclusion, it deserves deeper analysis. Still, the empirical result stands, so this is a framing and justification issue rather than a technical error.

comments.title: Claim that IS-A and IS-B increase scores faster but are not optimal for small tasks is under-supported
comments.quote: IS-A and IS-B increase top scores slightly faster than other methods... This indicates that composite methods’ way of using parameterized models to replace computational procedures is not the optimal solution for small-scale tasks.
comments.explanation: The authors observe that IS-A and IS-B (forward modeling, not composite) accelerate early performance on TfBind and conclude that composite methods (IPS, IPR) are not optimal for small tasks. However, Table 3 shows IPS-A and IPS-B achieve the highest AUC for all three tasks (UTR, AMP, Fluo), including best final performance. In TfBind, while IS variants rise faster, the composite methods still achieve strong results. The term 'composite methods' refers to IPS and IPR, which combine multiple modeling strategies, but IS-A/B are not composite—they are pure forward modeling. The leap from 'non-composite methods rise faster' to 'composite modeling is suboptimal' conflates categories. The argument would be stronger if they compared IPS vs IS directly on TfBind. As written, it overreaches: faster early convergence doesn't imply that composite methods are suboptimal. Therefore, the claim that Example B is 'not usable' in IR seems unjustified. The rejection of the second variant may reflect a design choice (e.g., to avoid temperature tuning) rather than a fundamental incompatibility. This is a logical gap: the justification provided is insufficient and contradicts standard practice in likelihood-free inference.

comments.title: Inconsistency in posterior target between text and Algorithm 3 for SNP
comments.explanation: The objective in Algorithm 3 reads: minimize KL(p(θ|x) || q), but this is not standard. In neural posterior estimation (e.g., SNPE or sequential neural posterior), the typical objective is to minimize the KL between the approximate posterior q_φ(θ|x) and the true posterior p(θ|x), which leads to maximum likelihood training on the role-reversed dataset. However, the KL divergence is written here in the reverse direction—KL(p || q)—which is not variationally tractable because p is intractable. This suggests a typo: it should be min_φ KL(q_φ(θ|x) || p(θ|x)), or equivalently, maximizing the log-likelihood E[log q_φ(θ|x)]. The surrounding text says 'minimizing the KL divergence between qφ(θ|x) and the true posterior p(θ|x)', which correctly describes the forward KL. Therefore, the written objective in the algorithm pseudocode contradicts the textual description. This is not an OCR error: 'KL(_p_(**_θ_**_|_**x**)_∥q_)' clearly has _p_ as the first argument. Given that variation inference universally uses KL(q||p), and that optimizing KL(p||q) would require samples from p and is not feasible here, the objective as written is infeasible. The authors likely intended the standard form. This is a genuine formal error in the pseudocode that could mislead implementation.

comments.title: Missing normalization or sampling step in forward modeling (IS and SNL)
comments.quote: The downside of this forward approach is an additional computational Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) step is needed to sample from the _r_ -th round posterior / proposal _pr_ ( _**θ**_ ).
comments.explanation: The text correctly notes that SNL requires MCMC to sample from the posterior due to the lack of an explicit sampler. However, when transitioning to the analog algorithm IS, there is no mention of how _pr_+1(**m**) = ˜_q_(**m**) is sampled from, especially when ˜_q_(**m**) is defined via an unnormalized density (e.g., p(**m**) exp(ˆ_f_φ(**m**)/τ)). The algorithm says 'construct ˜_q_(**m**)' and sets _pr_+1 ← ˜_q_, but offers no detail on how to sample from it in the next round. Unlike SNL, which explicitly uses MCMC, IS appears to assume that sampling from ˜_q_ is straightforward—perhaps via rejection sampling or an implicit generator—but this is not specified. Given that the forward model ˆ_f_φ is a regressor (not a generative model), and ˜_q_ is only defined up to normalization, sampling from it is non-trivial. The omission raises reproducibility concerns: a reader would not know whether to use MCMC, rejection sampling, or another method. While this may be detailed in the appendix (Section A.2), it is underspecified in the main method description. Since sampling is essential to the algorithm's loop, this missing component constitutes a methodological gap.

comments.title: Inconsistent posterior update in Algorithm 4 vs. Algorithm 3
comments.explanation: At first glance, the update in Algorithm 4 appears symmetric to Algorithm 3: both perform density estimation via KL minimization and set the next proposal to the estimated density. However, upon closer inspection, Algorithm 3 updates pr+1(θ) ← qφ(θ|x_o), which conditions on the observed data x_o, while Algorithm 4 sets pr+1(m) ← qφ(m) without conditioning on E. This breaks the correspondence unless qφ(m) is explicitly conditioned on E. The text says that qφ models p(m|E), but since E is not an input to qφ during training or sampling (unlike x in SNP), the model cannot adapt to different events E without retraining. In the LFI setting (Algorithm 3), the neural posterior estimator is trained on (θ_i, x_i) pairs and can evaluate p(θ|x_o) for any x_o; in contrast, Algorithm 4's qφ only sees sequences m_i (and possibly s_i), but not the event E directly. This creates a mismatch: the framework assumes E is fixed during training (e.g., via threshold s in Example A), so the algorithm is not general across different E. While this might be acceptable for fixed tasks, it undermines the claim of a unifying framework unless the conditioning structure is made equivalent. The authors correctly mirror the structure, but the implementation asymmetry weakens the analogy.

comments.title: Ambiguity in probability ratio definition for IR
comments.explanation: The text defines r(m) = p(m|E)/p(m), which by Bayes’ rule should be proportional to p(E|m)/p(E). However, the classifier dφ(m) is trained to distinguish m ∼ p(m|E) from m ∼ p(m), and standard classification theory tells us that the optimal d*(m) = p(D=m|E)/[p(D=m|E)+p(D=m)], assuming equal priors on the two classes. But in IR, D and D′ are not balanced: D contains sequences from the proposal with oracle scores, while D̃ is constructed from sequences satisfying E, and D̃′ is drawn from p(m). The loss in Algorithm 8 does not specify class weights, so the resulting dφ(m) will only recover the density ratio if the training distribution matches the class balance. The text does not clarify whether this balance is enforced. Moreover, in the update pr+1(m) ∝ rφ(m) · p(m), this should yield p(m|E), but only if rφ(m) estimates p(m|E)/p(m) correctly — which depends on the classifier being trained with balanced classes or properly weighted loss. Without such clarification, a reader could misinterpret when the method is valid. Context from SNR helps, but the asymmetry in data construction (D′ is synthetic while D is empirical) is not fully resolved.

comments.title: Missing formula in Example B Boltzmann definition
comments.quote:
In a softer version of _E_ and _C_ , we can define its conditional probability of being true to follow a Boltzmann distribution:

where _τ_ is a temperature parameter.
comments.explanation: The text states that the conditional probability follows a Boltzmann distribution and references a formula, but no equation is visible in the passage. Given the context and Example A, the intended definition is likely p(E|m) = exp(f(m)/τ) / Z or similar, but the omission makes it impossible to verify the correctness of later derivations (e.g., in IS-B or IR). The surrounding discussion in the full paper context implies p(E|m) ∝ exp(f(m)/τ), but without the explicit formula, readers cannot assess whether normalization or monotonicity assumptions hold. This is a clear presentation gap — the formula is logically necessary and must appear just after this sentence. While the idea is clear from prose, the absence undermines reproducibility and precise analysis, especially for the connection to likelihood-free ratio estimation.

comments.title: Confusing mapping T interpretation in text
comments.quote:
Another way to understand this correspondence is to consider the following mapping _T_ :

Here we can see the score value _s_ as a quantitative metric for how close the generated data **x** (given _**θ**_ ) is to the target observed data **x** _o_ . In addition, querying the oracle in the sequence design setting can also be thought of as follows: (1) sample **x** _∼ p_ ( _·|_ _**θ**_ ) and then (2) calculate _s_ = _−∥_ **x** _−_ **x** _o∥_ under some distance _∥·∥_ .
comments.explanation: Initially, the mapping T is presented as a way to transform LFI into black-box optimization. However, in most LFI problems, x is high-dimensional and complex (e.g., image or sequence), while in sequence design, m is also a sequence and f(m) is a scalar score. The suggestion that s = −‖x − x_o‖ implies that the oracle score is directly a negative distance from observed data — which would only make sense if the sequence design task is reconstruction, not optimization of general properties (e.g., binding affinity). This is misleading unless clarified. The correspondence holds more generally when f(m) encodes fitness and x_o is implicitly defined via a loss, but the example makes it seem that all sequence design must be inverse problems reconstructing x_o. The authors correctly note it's conceptual, but the specific instantiation may cause a careful reader to question the generality of the framework. This overinterpretation of T as a universal construction weakens the abstraction, though the core idea remains valid.

comments.title: Inconsistent variable in IR proposal update
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 8, the proposal update line reads 'pr+1(**θ**) ∝ rφ(**m**) · p(**m**)', which mixes variables: the left side uses θ, but the right side uses m. Given that this is a sequence design algorithm (IR), all instances should be m. This is likely an OCR or copy-paste error from Algorithm 7 (SNR), where θ is correct. The surrounding context (D built on m_i, p(m), etc.) confirms that m is the correct variable. While a reader would immediately infer the intent, the inconsistency could momentarily confuse, especially when comparing across algorithms. Since θ and m are structurally analogous but semantically distinct, this typo risks misalignment in the mapping the authors strive to clarify. Correcting to pr+1(**m**) restores consistency.

comments.title: Inconsistent proposal update in Iterative Ratio (Algorithm 8)
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 8 (Iterative Ratio), the update step for the next-round proposal uses the symbol **_θ_** on the left-hand side (`pr_+1(**_θ_**)`) but defines the update in terms of **m**, `rφ_(**m**)`, and `p_(**m**)`. This is inconsistent notation: the left side suggests the proposal is over parameters **_θ_** (as in LFI), but the right side clearly refers to sequence space **m**, indicating this should be `pr_+1(**m**)`. Given the full context—especially the clear mapping between LFI and sequence design in Table 1 and Section 2.2—the intent is unambiguous: the update is meant to be over sequences **m**. However, the use of **_θ_** here is a clear notational inconsistency that could mislead a reader, particularly when comparing across algorithm columns. The left column uses proper LFI notation (**_θ_**), so the right column should mirror it with **m**, not mix domains. This appears to be either a typo or OCR error, but even if OCR-related, the resulting symbol clashes with the established definition of `pr(·)` in the sequence setting.

comments.title: Ambiguity in construction of ˜D and ˜D′ in IR and IPR algorithms
comments.quote: construct ˜_D_with**m**in_D_satisfying_E_;
comments.explanation: The instruction 'construct ˜_D with **m** in _D_ satisfying _E_' is underspecified. While from context (e.g., Example A defining _E_ via a score threshold), we can infer that ˜_D_ is the subset of _D_ where _f(m_i) ≥ s_, the value of the threshold _s_ is not specified here. Is _s_ fixed, adaptive (e.g., top-k), or quantile-based? The same issue arises in IPR (Algorithm 10). The lack of specification makes it impossible to reproduce the method as written. Although later experimental details mention batch sizes and rounds, they do not clarify how _E_ is concretely instantiated in the algorithm. This is particularly important because IR is said to only support the hard threshold (Example A), yet the thresholding rule is not defined in the algorithm itself. While the paper references Appendix A.3, the main algorithm should minimally specify how _E_ is operationalized (e.g., via a quantile or fixed value). This underspecification affects reproducibility and is not resolved by surrounding context.

comments.title: Missing normalization clarification in ratio-based posterior construction
comments.quote: construct ˜_q_(**m**)with_rφ_(**m**)and_p_(**m**);
comments.explanation: In both Algorithm 8 (IR) and Algorithm 10 (IPR), after estimating the ratio _rφ_(**m**) = d(**m**)/(1−d(**m**)), the algorithm constructs ˜_q_(**m**) using _rφ_(**m**) and _p_(**m**). While the authors note in the text that IR does not support the soft definition (Example B) due to intractable normalization, it is not explicitly stated how ˜_q_(**m**) is constructed in the hard threshold case. From context, one can infer ˜_q_(**m**) ∝ rφ(**m**) p(**m**), but the lack of definition here is problematic. In contrast, IS-B explicitly defines ˜q(**m**) ∝ p(**m**) exp(f_φ(**m**)/τ). The asymmetry in specification leaves the reader to guess whether the ratio-based ˜q is normalized, sampled directly, or used in a weighted fashion. This is especially important in IPR where the KL minimization depends on having a well-defined ˜q. The omission is not trivial because the ratio estimation via classification yields unnormalized importance weights, and their use in KL minimization requires care. This constitutes underspecification of a core step.

comments.title: Inconsistent naming of estimated score function across algorithms
comments.quote: ft ˆ_fφ_(**m**)with_D_;
comments.explanation: The notation `ˆ_fφ_` (likely intended to be \hat{f}_\phi) is used in Algorithms 6, 9, and elsewhere to denote the trained regressor approximating the oracle score. However, in the paper summary and equation list, the symbol is consistently given as `f_φ(m)` without the hat. While the hat might intend to emphasize estimation, its inconsistent use (present in algorithms but absent in key equations like ˜q(m) ∝ p(m) · exp(f_φ(m)/τ)) creates ambiguity about whether `\hat{f}_\phi` and `f_φ` denote different objects. Given that the regressor is an approximation, the hat is semantically appropriate, but its sporadic appearance—especially in algorithm pseudocode while absent in formal definitions—introduces a notation inconsistency. This could confuse readers attempting to map algorithmic steps to theoretical formulations. The OCR may have introduced formatting noise (e.g., `ˆ_fφ_`), but the core issue is the mismatch between algorithmic and analytical notation for the same object.

comments.title: Unclear status of dataset D′ in Algorithm 8 (IR)
comments.quote: construct ˜_D′_ from_p_(**m**);
comments.explanation: The instruction 'construct ˜_D′ from p(**m**)’ lacks detail. How many samples? Are they newly drawn each round? Is the size matched to ˜_D_? In noise contrastive estimation and SNR, the contrastive samples (D′) are typically drawn from a noise distribution with matched size to the data samples for stable training. In IR, since ˜_D_ is a filtered subset of _D_, its size varies per round. For fair classification, ˜_D′_ should likely match that size. But this is unstated. The paper mentions D′ is 'used in IR for contrastive training' in the summary, but the algorithm must specify sampling procedure. Without this, the method is underspecified. While Assumption 7 mentions iterative updates, it doesn’t resolve the sampling strategy. This affects balance in the binary classification and thus the quality of the ratio estimate—a key component. Hence, despite the conceptual clarity of contrastive learning, the implementation detail is missing.

comments.title: Mismatch in proposal update variable in Algorithm 8
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 8 (Iterative Ratio), the final update step reads `pr+1(**θ**) ∝ rφ(**m**) · p(**m**)`, which uses **θ** on the left-hand side but **m** on the right. This is inconsistent with the rest of the paper, where **m** denotes biological sequences and **θ** denotes LFI parameters. Given that this is the sequence design version of SNR, the proposal should be over **m**, not **θ**. Initially, I considered whether this was an OCR error (e.g., **m** misread as **θ**), but the surrounding code uses **m** correctly, and **θ** appears only here. The rest of the IR description treats **m** as the object being sampled and updated. The mismatch suggests a typographical error in the LaTeX, likely meant to be `pr+1(**m**) ∝ rφ(**m**) · p(**m**)`. This kind of error is common when adapting template algorithms, but left uncorrected, it could confuse a reader implementing the method. The intent is clear from context (the algorithm is for sequence design), but the notation is formally inconsistent.

comments.title: Missing construction of ˜D′ in Algorithm 8 description
comments.quote: construct ˜_D′_ from_p_(**m**);
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 8, the step 'construct ˜D′ from p(**m**)’ is underspecified. From the paper’s framework, ˜D′ should be a dataset of sequences sampled from the prior p(**m**) to contrast with ˜D (sequences from D satisfying E). However, the size of ˜D′, sampling strategy, and whether it’s refreshed per round are not specified. In contrast, in the IR section text, it's mentioned that D′ is used for contrastive training, but the algorithm doesn’t clarify how ˜D′ relates to D′ or how many samples are drawn. The analogous SNR (Algorithm 7) samples n pairs for D′ using the same proposal pr(θ), but IR does not specify whether ˜D′ is sampled from the prior p(**m**) or the current proposal pr(**m**), nor the batch size. Since the prior p(**m**) may be simple (e.g., uniform), sampling from it makes sense, but this should be explicit. The lack of detail hinders reproducibility, especially since using the proposal vs. prior affects classifier calibration. This is a real gap in method description, not just a formatting issue.

comments.title: Claim that backward modeling is superior in Fluo task contradicts LFI literature without explanation
comments.quote: This might signify that for long sequence design tasks, backward modeling is superior to other modeling methods, which is not consistent with LFI (Papamakarios et al., 2019).
comments.explanation: The authors observe that DbAS (a backward modeling method) performs well on the Fluo task and suggest this contradicts the LFI literature (Papamakarios et al., 2019), which claims forward modeling is easier. However, they do not justify why this reversal might occur. While forward modeling (e.g., SNL) avoids dependence on the proposal, backward modeling (e.g., SNP) directly models the posterior, which may be more effective when the posterior is sharp or when the sequence space is extremely high-dimensional (as in Fluo: 20^238). The paper could have discussed potential reasons—e.g., that direct posterior modeling benefits from focusing on high-score regions as the proposal improves, or that the high dimensionality favors amortized posterior estimation. As it stands, the claim is presented as an observation without mechanistic justification, making it feel like a post-hoc rationalization. Given that this is a key insight in the conclusion, it deserves deeper analysis. Still, the empirical result stands, so this is a framing and justification issue rather than a technical error.

comments.title: Table 3 contains negative values for Fluo, but Fluo is a fluorescence intensity regression task
comments.quote: −5.67, −5.11, ..., −5.54 in Table 3 under FLUO
comments.explanation: Table 3 reports negative values (e.g., −5.67) for the Fluo benchmark, which is described as a fluorescence intensity prediction task using a regressor predicting log-fluorescence. While log-fluorescence can be negative (indicating sub-unity intensity), the term 'area under top-100 curves' suggests a cumulative score that should be positive if it aggregates top scores. However, if the 'top score' itself is on a log scale (e.g., log-fluorescence), then integrating negative values could yield a negative AUC. The paper does not clarify whether the y-axis in Figure 2 (right) is raw log-fluorescence or a transformed score. If the scores are indeed negative, using 'top score' to describe them is confusing—typically, fluorescence optimization aims to maximize brightness, so one would expect positive gains. The issue is not necessarily an error but a lack of clarity: either the oracle outputs negative values (plausible), or the table reports something other than score (e.g., improvement over baseline). Without clarification, a reader might question the validity of negative 'top scores'. Given the context, it’s likely correct (log-scale), but the presentation risks confusion.

comments.title: Inconsistency in search space size for TfBind task
comments.quote: The size of the search space is |V|^L = 4^8 = 65536.
comments.explanation: The text states |V|^L = 4^8 = 65536 for TfBind, but 4^8 is 65,536, which is correctly stated. However, in scientific writing, large numbers are often formatted with commas (65,536), but the absence is not an error. More importantly, the value is correct: 4 nucleotides, 8 positions, 4^8 = 65536. This is consistent with standard combinatorics. No issue here after verification. Initially, I double-checked the arithmetic due to the unusual number format, but it’s accurate. Not a real issue.

comments.title: Claim that IS-A and IS-B increase scores faster but are not optimal for small tasks is under-supported
comments.quote: IS-A and IS-B increase top scores slightly faster than other methods... This indicates that composite methods’ way of using parameterized models to replace computational procedures is not the optimal solution for small-scale tasks.
comments.explanation: The authors observe that IS-A and IS-B (forward modeling, not composite) accelerate early performance on TfBind and conclude that composite methods (IPS, IPR) are not optimal for small tasks. However, Table 3 shows IPS-A and IPS-B achieve the highest AUC for all three tasks (UTR, AMP, Fluo), including best final performance. In TfBind, while IS variants rise faster, the composite methods still achieve strong results. The term 'composite methods' refers to IPS and IPR, which combine multiple modeling strategies, but IS-A/B are not composite—they are pure forward modeling. The leap from 'non-composite methods rise faster' to 'composite modeling is suboptimal' conflates categories. The argument would be stronger if they compared IPS vs IS directly on TfBind. As written, it overreaches: faster early convergence doesn't imply that composite methods are suboptimal. This is a logical overstatement based on limited evidence, though the core observation (early advantage of IS) may be valid.

progressive__glm-4.6.label: Progressive (glm-4.6)
progressive__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper presents an ambitious and potentially impactful work by proposing a novel probabilistic framework to unify likelihood-free inference (LFI) and black-box optimization (BBO) for biological sequence design. The motivation is clearly articulated, effectively positioning the work against existing methods and highlighting the critical need for sample-efficient algorithms in this domain. The central thesis—reformulating BBO as a posterior modeling problem to establish a conceptual bridge to LFI—is insightful and provides a principled foundation for algorithm design. However, the most significant issue emerging from the initial sections is the potential gap between this high-level conceptual unification and its concrete algorithmic implications. While the framework is presented as a "recipe" for developing new methods, the text does not yet elucidate how this probabilistic reformulation overcomes the fundamental challenges of BBO or leads to more sample-efficient algorithms compared to established paradigms like Bayesian optimization. The clarity of the writing is strong, but the paper's ultimate success will hinge on its ability to convincingly demonstrate that this elegant theoretical connection translates into practical, performance-driven advantages in the subsequent sections.

comments.title: Missing equation in Example B
comments.quote:
**Example B.** In a softer version of _E_ and _C_ , we can define its conditional probability of being true to follow a Boltzmann distribution:

where _τ_ is a temperature parameter.
comments.explanation: The passage introduces Example B with a Boltzmann distribution but the actual equation defining the distribution is missing from the text. This appears to be an OCR error where the mathematical expression wasn't captured. The text continues with the explanation of the temperature parameter τ, but without seeing the actual Boltzmann distribution formula, readers cannot fully understand this alternative formulation to Example A. This is significant because Example B is referenced later in the algorithms (IS-B variant).

comments.title: Missing definition of mapping T
comments.quote:
Another way to understand this correspondence is to consider the following mapping _T_ :

Here we can see the score value _s_ as a quantitative metric for how close the generated data **x** (given _**θ**_ ) is to the target observed data **x** _o_ .
comments.explanation: The text introduces a mapping T as an alternative way to understand the correspondence between LFI and black-box optimization, but the actual definition of T is missing from the passage. This appears to be an OCR error where the mathematical definition wasn't captured. The explanatory text that follows refers to this mapping, making its absence particularly problematic since the authors claim this mapping 'could conceptually transform any LFI problem into a black-box optimization task' - a significant claim that cannot be evaluated without seeing the actual mapping.

comments.title: OCR error in Example B explanation
comments.quote: We introduce the exponential because _f_ ( _·_ ) does not necessarily _·_ take positive values.
comments.explanation: There appears to be an extra dot/period in the phrase 'does not necessarily · take positive values'. This is likely an OCR error where a mathematical symbol (possibly a multiplication dot) was incorrectly inserted. The intended meaning is clear from context - that the function f(·) may produce negative values, which is why the exponential transformation is needed for the Boltzmann distribution.

comments.title: Incomplete Proposition 1 statement
comments.quote:
**Proposition 1.** _Let p_ 0( **a** ) _and p_ 1( **a** ) _be two distributions for da-dimension random variable_ **a** _which takes value in the space of A_ = R _[d][a] , and d_ ( **a** ) : _A →_ [0 _,_ 1] _is a real-value function mapping any_ **a** _to a positive real value number. Then the functional optimization problem_

_will lead to the optimal solution d[∗]_ ( **a** ) =
comments.explanation: The proposition is incomplete - it states 'Then the functional optimization problem' but doesn't actually specify what the optimization problem is. Based on the conclusion and context, it should be minimizing the cross-entropy loss: min_d E_{p1}[-log d(a)] + E_{p0}[-log(1-d(a))], but this crucial formulation is missing, making the proposition incomprehensible as stated.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in Proposition 1
comments.quote: **Proposition 1.** _Let p_ 0( **a** ) _and p_ 1( **a** ) _be two distributions for da-dimension random variable_ **a** _which takes value in the space of A_ = R _[d][a]
comments.explanation: The notation is inconsistent. It first says 'da-dimension' suggesting d_a is subscript, then uses 'R_[d][a]' which appears to be OCR garbled. Based on standard mathematical notation and the context, this should likely be 'd_a-dimension' and 'R^{d_a}' (d_a-dimensional Euclidean space).

comments.title: Garbled sentence about constructing ˜q(m)
comments.quote: Inˆ the spirit of directly modeling the forward mechanism of sequence design, we train a regressor procedure of the proposal of the next round, we use _fφ_ ( **m** ) in a supervised manner to fit the oracle scorer. _q_ ˜(In order to adapt this regressor into the update **m** ) to denote the unknown posterior _p_ ( **m** _|E_ ) ofwith knowledge of _E_ .
comments.explanation: This sentence appears to have OCR errors and is difficult to parse. It seems to be trying to explain: (1) they train a regressor fφ(m) to fit the oracle, and (2) they use ˜q(m) to denote the posterior p(m|E) when constructing the next proposal. The phrase 'ofwith knowledge of E' is particularly unclear.

comments.title: Ambiguous scope of ˜q(m) construction
comments.quote: The specific construction of ˜ _q_ ( **m** ) depends on the choice _E_ , then _q_ ˜( **m** ) is the distribution with (unnormalized) probability _p_ ( **m** ) _·_ exp( _f_[ˆ] _φ_ ( **m** ) _/τ_ ).
comments.explanation: The text states that ˜q(m) construction depends on the choice of E, but then only provides the formula for Example B (Boltzmann distribution case). It's unclear whether this formula applies generally or only to Example B. The sentence structure suggests it might be claiming this is the general construction, which would be incorrect since Example A uses a hard threshold.

comments.title: Variable typo in Algorithm 8 update step
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 8 (Iterative Ratio), the last line of the main loop incorrectly uses θ instead of m in the proposal update. The algorithm is clearly for sequence design (using m throughout), and the ratio r_φ(m) and prior p(m) are correctly defined in terms of m. This appears to be a copy-paste error from Algorithm 7, as it should read pr+1(m) ∝ r_φ(m) · p(m) to be consistent with the rest of the algorithm and the context of sequence design.

comments.title: Underspecified dataset construction in Algorithm 8
comments.quote: construct ˜_D_with**m**in_D_satisfying_E_;\nconstruct ˜_D′_ from_p_(**m**);
comments.explanation: Algorithm 8's construction of the datasets ˜D and ˜D' for training the classifier is insufficiently specified. While ˜D should contain sequences from D that satisfy event E, the exact criteria and method for selection are unclear. Similarly, 'construct ˜D' from p(m)' is vague - does this mean sampling from the prior? How many samples? What's the relationship to D? This lack of detail makes the algorithm difficult to reproduce and understand properly.

comments.title: Unconventional sampling in Algorithm 7
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 7, both θ_i and θ'_i are sampled from the same proposal distribution pr(θ). This is unconventional for Sequential Neural Ratio methods, which typically sample θ_i from the current proposal and θ'_i from the prior to create a proper contrastive learning setup. While this could be a specific variant, it deviates from standard SNR implementations without explanation or justification in the text.

comments.title: Mathematical notation for search space sizes
comments.quote: The size of the search space is _|V|[L]_ = 4[8] = 65536... The length of the gene sequences is fixed to 50, and thus the size of the search space is 4[50]... The size of search space is[][60] _L_ =12[20] _[L]_[.][We][set]... This task's search space is 20[238]
comments.explanation: The mathematical notation for search space sizes is inconsistent and contains errors. In the TfBind section, '4[8]' should be '4^8' (4 to the power of 8). In the UTR section, '4[50]' should be '4^{50}'. In the AMP section, the expression '[][60] _L_ =12[20] _[L]_[.][We][set]' appears to be OCR corruption of what should be '∑_{L=12}^{60} 20^L'. In the Fluo section, '20[238]' should be '20^{238}'. These notation errors could confuse readers about the actual combinatorial complexity of the search spaces.

comments.title: Table 3 formatting and clarity
comments.explanation: Table 3 has formatting issues that make it difficult to read. The vertical bars and underscore formatting (e.g., '8_._46' instead of '8.46') create confusion. The table structure appears to be meant to show area under top-100 curves for three benchmarks (UTR, AMP, FLUO) across different methods, but the current formatting makes it hard to parse which values correspond to which benchmark and method. This could lead to misinterpretation of the comparative results.

progressive_original__glm-4.6.label: Progressive Original (glm-4.6)
progressive_original__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper presents an ambitious and potentially impactful work by proposing a novel probabilistic framework to unify likelihood-free inference (LFI) and black-box optimization (BBO) for biological sequence design. The motivation is clearly articulated, effectively positioning the work against existing methods and highlighting the critical need for sample-efficient algorithms in this domain. The central thesis—reformulating BBO as a posterior modeling problem to establish a conceptual bridge to LFI—is insightful and provides a principled foundation for algorithm design. However, the most significant issue emerging from the initial sections is the potential gap between this high-level conceptual unification and its concrete algorithmic implications. While the framework is presented as a "recipe" for developing new methods, the text does not yet elucidate how this probabilistic reformulation overcomes the fundamental challenges of BBO or leads to more sample-efficient algorithms compared to established paradigms like Bayesian optimization. The clarity of the writing is strong, but the paper's ultimate success will hinge on its ability to convincingly demonstrate that this elegant theoretical connection translates into practical, performance-driven advantages in the subsequent sections.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The paper presents a sophisticated and theoretically elegant proposal to bridge the gap between likelihood-free inference (LFI) and black-box optimization, offering a compelling paradigm shift for biological sequence design. The clarity of the motivation—transitioning from point-estimation optimization to posterior modeling—is a notable strength, as it effectively addresses the sample-efficiency constraints inherent in wet-lab experimentation. However, despite the strong conceptual framing, the paper’s primary challenge lies in the pedagogical burden of justifying this "unification." A critical reader may find the jump from LFI to sequence design abstract; the authors must ensure that the mathematical mapping between $p(\theta|x)$ and the optimization objective $p(m|E)$ is not merely formalistic but provides tangible algorithmic advantages over existing Bayesian optimization or active learning baselines. If the subsequent sections can demonstrate that this framework yields novel, high-performing "composite" algorithms that are distinctly more robust than standard methods, the work will serve as a significant contribution to both machine learning theory and drug discovery applications.

comments.title: Missing mathematical definition for LFI objective
comments.quote: Consider the objective of modeling the Bayesian posterior when we cannot compute the likelihood p( x o| θ ):
comments.explanation: The introduction to LFI ends abruptly without providing the foundational mathematical identity p(θ|x_o) = p(x_o|θ)p(θ) / p(x_o). Providing this formula is necessary to establish the theoretical framework for the algorithms that follow.

comments.title: Severe text fragmentation and OCR errors in Section 3.2
comments.quote: In  the spirit of directly modeling the forward mechanism of sequence design, we train a regressor procedure of the proposal of the next round...
comments.explanation: The block describing 'Iterative Scoring' contains massive OCR-induced corruption, including transposed sentences and missing syntax. This renders the core algorithm description unintelligible and impossible to implement or verify.

comments.title: Ambiguity and inconsistency regarding normalization of the Boltzmann distribution
comments.quote: We point out that IR does not have two variants as IS does, as the definition for E in Example B is not usable because of the unknown normalizing constant for probability p( E|m ).
comments.explanation: The authors claim that the intractable partition function Z in the Boltzmann distribution prevents the use of IR/IPR. This contradicts the fundamental principle of Noise Contrastive Estimation (NCE) and Ratio estimation, where the normalizing constant is implicitly handled. The rationale requires significant clarification to explain why this specific application differs from standard NCE properties.

comments.title: Algorithmic underspecification and notation errors in Algorithm 8
comments.quote: construct ˜q(m) with ˆfφ(·) and p(m); ... pr+1(θ) ∝ rφ(m) · p(m);
comments.explanation: Algorithm 8 contains multiple defects: it fails to specify hyperparameters for the prior sampling frequency (dataset size for ˜D'), uses inconsistent notation (θ instead of m), and incorrectly returns a literal for likelihood-free inference (p(θ|xo)) rather than the required sequence output. These flaws obscure the procedural details for researchers attempting to reproduce the method.

comments.explanation: The prose describing the Iterative Posterior Ratio (IPR) method contradicts both the logic of the algorithm and the provided pseudocode in Algorithm 10. The text suggests that the intermediate target q˜(m) is not the goal, while the training objective and pseudocode imply it is central to the KL divergence minimization.

comments.explanation: Multiple instances of garbled character sequences (e.g., '~~S~~', '~~R~~', '~~E~~') appear in task and protein variant names throughout the results section. This obscures the identity of the datasets, hindering reader verification and reproducibility.

progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.overall_feedback: The paper presents a sophisticated and theoretically elegant proposal to bridge the gap between likelihood-free inference (LFI) and black-box optimization, offering a compelling paradigm shift for biological sequence design. The clarity of the motivation—transitioning from point-estimation optimization to posterior modeling—is a notable strength, as it effectively addresses the sample-efficiency constraints inherent in wet-lab experimentation. However, despite the strong conceptual framing, the paper’s primary challenge lies in the pedagogical burden of justifying this "unification." A critical reader may find the jump from LFI to sequence design abstract; the authors must ensure that the mathematical mapping between $p(\theta|x)$ and the optimization objective $p(m|E)$ is not merely formalistic but provides tangible algorithmic advantages over existing Bayesian optimization or active learning baselines. If the subsequent sections can demonstrate that this framework yields novel, high-performing "composite" algorithms that are distinctly more robust than standard methods, the work will serve as a significant contribution to both machine learning theory and drug discovery applications.

comments.title: Formula missing for LFI posterior objective
comments.quote: Consider the objective of modeling the Bayesian posterior when we cannot compute the likelihood p( x o| θ ):
comments.explanation: The text introducing LFI ends with a colon but fails to provide the mathematical expression for the posterior p(θ|x_o) = p(x_o|θ)p(θ) / p(x_o) before proceeding to the text. While this is a standard identity, the author explicitly sets up the objective formula but leaves it blank, creating a significant break in the flow of the mathematical derivation which should be the foundation for the rest of the paper.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of M
comments.quote: In our setting, we use M = V[L] to denote the search space
comments.explanation: The notation M appears to be an OCR misread or a notation error for the set of sequences of length L, typically written as V^L. In the later part of the same paragraph, it is correctly written as V^{L} (or implied by the context of V^L). Treating 'M' as the set itself while using subscript notation V[L] is confusing; additionally, the subsequent mention uses V^{L} again for variable length. This is likely an OCR conversion artifact where the superscript was lost, but it should be flagged as it obscures the definition of the search space.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Boltzmann-weighted posterior formulation
comments.quote: p(E|m) = exp(f(m)/τ) / Z
comments.explanation: In Section 2.2, Example B, the text states a Boltzmann distribution for p(E|m) but does not define the normalizing constant Z (or whether it is a function of m). Since the target posterior p(m|E) ∝ p(m)p(E|m), the inclusion of an arbitrary normalizing constant in p(E|m) is mathematically loose. While context implies this is a soft-max-like weighting, the lack of explicit treatment of the partition function—which is typically intractable in black-box settings—makes the theoretical derivation of p(m|E) slightly underspecified, particularly for readers attempting to reconcile this with the subsequent ratio-based methods (IR).

comments.title: Implicit assumption on oracle noise in Algorithm 6
comments.quote: construct ˜q(m) with ˆfφ(·) and p(m)
comments.explanation: Algorithm 6 (Iterative Scoring) describes using a regressor ˆfφ(m) to construct a proposal ˜q(m). The text mentions Example B as a way to construct this, but does not specify how the temperature parameter τ (from Example B) or the scaling of the output of ˆfφ relates to the proposal distribution update. In an iterative setting, if the proposal is meant to track a distribution that sharpens towards high-scoring regions, the update rule needs to clarify if it is a tempered distribution or a simple weighted re-sampling. The current description is underspecified for a reader trying to implement the algorithm.

comments.title: Missing definition for the classifier loss in Algorithm 8
comments.quote: train dφ(m) classifying between ˜D and ˜D′
comments.explanation: The text refers to a binary classification approach for Iterative Ratio (Algorithm 8) and mentions a loss in the paragraph above, but the specific loss function Eq. 4 is referenced in the context of Algorithm 7 (for likelihood-free inference on θ and x). It is not immediately clear if the loss for IR uses the exact same form (the standard Brier or Logarithmic loss for NCE) applied to m. While the mapping is intuitive, the formal objective should be explicitly restated for m to ensure the ratio rφ(m) is correctly identified as p(m|E)/p(m).

comments.title: Text Fragmentation in Section 3.2
comments.quote: In  the spirit of directly modeling the forward mechanism of sequence design, we train a regressor procedure of the proposal of the next round, we use $f\phi$ ( **m** ) in a supervised manner to fit the oracle scorer. $q$ \tilde(In order to adapt this regressor into the update **m** ) to denote the unknown posterior $p$ ( **m** $|E$ ) ofwith knowledge of $E$ . For instance, if we choose Example B in Section 2.2 to be the definition of $f[\hat{}] \phi$ ( **m** ) and prior $p$ ( **m** ).
comments.explanation: The text block appears to have suffered from severe OCR-induced fragmentation and insertion errors, rendering the description of the 'Iterative Scoring' algorithm (IS) methodology unintelligible. Phrases like 'we train a regressor procedure of the proposal of the next round, we use $f\phi$ ( **m** ) in a supervised manner' and the jumbled '$\tilde{q}$ (In order to adapt...' make the definition of the algorithm's update mechanism impossible to follow. While the intent is to describe forward modeling, the procedural details required for reproduction are lost.

comments.title: Unclear Definition of Normalizing Constant in Iterative Ratio (IR)
comments.quote: We point out that IR does not have two variants as IS does, as the definition for $E$ in Example B is not usable because of the unknown normalizing constant for probability $p$ ( $E|\mathbf{m}$ ).
comments.explanation: The authors state that Example B (Boltzmann distribution) cannot be used for IR due to an 'unknown normalizing constant'. However, in binary classification (Noise Contrastive Estimation), the exact normalizing constant of the target distribution is typically not required for the ratio estimator to converge, as the discriminant $d(\mathbf{m})$ learns the ratio relative to the noise distribution (prior $p(\mathbf{m})$). If the authors mean the target is $\exp(f(\mathbf{m})/\tau) / Z$, the constant $Z$ cancels out in the ratio $r(\mathbf{m}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{m}|E)}{p(\mathbf{m})}$. This explanation seems to misunderstand the standard property of NCE/SNR, which is specifically used to circumvent the need for knowing $Z$.

comments.title: Inconsistent objective function in text versus algorithm for IPR
comments.quote: construct ˜q(m) with rφ(m) and p(m); qψ ← arg minq DKL(˜q(m) ∥q); pr+1(m) ← qψ(m);
comments.explanation: In Section 3.4, the authors state regarding the Iterative Posterior Ratio (IPR) method: 'IPR works similarly to IR... but also adopts a second neural network model qψ(m) to model q˜(m) by minimizing KL divergence with q˜(m)'. However, defining the target distribution as q˜(m) ∝ rφ(m) · p(m) is consistent with the goal of posterior modeling. The confusion arises because in the text immediately preceding the algorithm, the authors state: 'the usage of two models in IPR is not q˜(m)... but the training of model dφ(m) is closer to that in IR'. This creates a contradiction where the prose describes the construction of q˜(m) using rφ(m) · p(m), while the snippet suggests an ambiguity about whether q˜(m) is the target of the KL divergence or merely an intermediate construct. Given that Algorithm 10 correctly implements the minimization of DKL(q˜(m) ∥ qψ), the prose is unnecessarily confusing and technically inconsistent with the provided pseudocode.

comments.title: Unclear handling of the normalization constant for Example B in IR/IPR
comments.quote: See Section A.3 in Appendix for more explanation.
comments.explanation: The authors state that Example B (Boltzmann distribution) is 'not usable because of the unknown normalizing constant for probability p(E|m)' in the context of IR/IPR. This is a significant claim because ratio estimation (like Noise Contrastive Estimation/SNR) is traditionally touted as a way to circumvent exact normalization by transforming the density ratio into a binary classification problem. While I understand the author's intent (that the target label for the classifier in IR/IPR usually requires sampling from a known distribution, and the Boltzmann distribution's normalizing constant is indeed intractable), it is not immediately clear why this prevents the use of the Boltzmann form, since the ratio r(m) = p(m|E)/p(m) = exp(f(m)/τ) / Z(τ) could theoretically be estimated. The justification provided is 'not usable', which seems to dismiss a potential path for these algorithms without sufficient mathematical explanation in the body.

comments.title: Missing definition of the dataset D' in Algorithm 8
comments.quote: construct ˜D with m in D satisfying E; construct ˜D' from p(m); train dφ(m) classifying between ˜D and ˜D';
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 8 (IR), the authors define dataset ˜D' as being constructed from p(m). However, there is no mention of the number of samples drawn from the prior per iteration for this dataset. While this is likely an implicit hyperparameter (the same as batch size n), in sequence design settings where oracle queries are expensive (a key constraint emphasized in the paper), the cost of sampling from the prior is negligible compared to the oracle. Clarifying that this dataset size is a design choice (potentially larger than n to improve classifier stability) would be helpful for reproducibility.
